title: Running Your Server
subtitle: Bring your server to life

sections.content:
Your server code is written, but right now it's just a file sitting on your computer. It's not *doing* anything yet.

When you run `node app.js`, something magical happens: your computer transforms from a regular machine into a **web server**. It starts *listening* for requests, just like a restaurant opens its doors and waits for customers.

The address it listens on is `localhost` — which literally means "this computer." Instead of serving pages to the whole internet, it serves them to *you*, right here, right now.
sections.analogy: Starting a server is like flipping the "OPEN" sign on a shop. The shop was always there, but now it's ready for visitors. Your computer was always there, but now it's ready to respond to web requests.
keyPoints: node app.js starts your server and keeps it running
The terminal will appear to "hang" — that's normal, your server is listening
localhost means your own computer is acting as the server

sections.instruction: Start your server by running the app.js file with Node. Make sure you're in your project directory (the folder with app.js and package.json).

troubleshooting.solution: You need to install Express first. Run: npm install express
Port 3000 is already in use. Either stop the other process using it, or change the port number in app.js to 3001.
There's a typo in your app.js file. Double-check the code from the previous lesson, especially quotes, parentheses, and semicolons.

sections.instruction:
Your server is now running! Open your web browser (Chrome, Safari, Firefox — any browser works) and type this address in the address bar:

**http://localhost:3000**

You should see your "Hello, World!" message displayed in the browser. That text is coming from your server — your computer received the browser's request and sent back a response.
sections.expectedOutput: Hello, World! This is my first server.

troubleshooting.problem: This site can't be reached / Connection refused
troubleshooting.solution: Make sure your server is still running in the terminal. If the terminal shows a regular prompt (not the 'Server running' message), run node app.js again.

troubleshooting.problem: The page loads but shows something different
troubleshooting.solution: Check that you're visiting http://localhost:3000 (not https, and make sure the port number matches what your app.js uses).

sections.content:
**You just built a web server. A real one.**

When you typed `localhost:3000` in your browser, here's what happened behind the scenes:

1. Your browser sent an **HTTP GET request** to your computer on port 3000
2. Your Express server received that request
3. The `app.get('/')` handler matched the URL
4. Your server sent back the text "Hello, World!"
5. Your browser displayed it

This is *exactly* how every website on the internet works. The only difference? Those servers run on big computers in data centers instead of your laptop. But the mechanism is identical.
keyPoints: Your browser is the client, your Node.js app is the server
This is the same request-response cycle that powers every website
Go back to your terminal — the server is still running, waiting for more requests

completionMessage: Your first server is alive! It's listening, it's responding, and it's running right on your machine. This is how every web developer starts.
